concreteImplOrErrorFound: Neither a concrete implementation nor a loud unsupported-error throw exists. All three dispatch paths still execute handlers exactly once with no retry/delay/concurrency enforcement, and the fields are silently ignored.
currentEvidence: packages/plugin-workers-core/src/runtime/job-dispatcher.ts:69-76 — dispatch() awaits resolve() then calls resolution.handler(context) exactly once; no retry loop, no retryDelay sleep, no concurrency semaphore.
packages/plugin-workers-core/src/runtime/in-process-job-runner.ts:24-34 — dispatch() only guards on #stopped then delegates to #dispatcher.dispatch once.
plugins/workers/worker/job-runner-pool.ts:46-65 — executeJob() generates an executionId and calls this.#runner.dispatch(jobDef, ...) a single time; no pool/concurrency limit honoring maxConcurrency.

plugins/workers/worker/job-dispatcher.ts:54-119 — processWorkerJob runs executeWorkerJob once inside traceJobExecution; maxRetries (line 61) is passed only as a trace attribute. The failure path recordJobFailure (lines 221-248) marks the execution failed/timeout and never re-attempts.
plugins/workers/worker/job-execution.ts:134-136 — copies maxRetries/retryDelay/maxConcurrency onto a synthesized TaskDefinition, but the consumer never reads them.

packages/plugin-workers-core/src/executor/multi-runtime-task-executor.ts:77-135 — execute() calls adapter.execute(task, resolved) once; #resolveOptions only resolves args/cwd/env/timeout — maxRetries/retryDelay/maxConcurrency are never consulted. TaskResult.attempt is hardcoded to 0 in adapters (runtime-adapter-base.ts:77, dax-process-runner.ts:174).

Public surface advertises these knobs: builders/job-builder.ts retry(maxRetries, options) with #maxRetries=3 default; domain/job-definition.ts schema defaults maxRetries:3, retryDelay:1000, maxConcurrency:1 — accepted, validated, persisted, and ignored at dispatch.
reasoning: Re-read of current main confirms no behavioral change on any dispatch path. InProcessJobDispatcher.dispatch (job-dispatcher.ts:69-76) invokes the handler exactly once. The plugin worker path (processWorkerJob -> executeWorkerJob -> WorkerPool.executeJob/MultiRuntimeTaskExecutor.execute) likewise executes once; maxRetries appears solely as a telemetry attribute, and MultiRuntimeTaskExecutor.#resolveOptions never reads retry/concurrency fields. No loud unsupported-operation error (no notImplemented(), no WorkersUnsupportedOperationError) was introduced, so this is not a reframe-as-deliberate-limitation. Because maxRetries/retryDelay/maxConcurrency are first-class, defaulted, validated builder/schema knobs presented in the public API, silently no-oping them on every job run is a correctness gap that should be implemented (retry-with-delay loop plus a concurrency gate) rather than merely documented.

There is also NO loud error throw — no StreamUnsupportedOperationError, notImplemented(), or unsupportedOperation guard — because there is simply no consume/subscribe entry point or method to call; the package surface omits the capability entirely rather than stubbing+throwing. This is a producer-only package whose missing consumer surface is explicitly documented as deferred scope. It is not a silent stub that returns fake data; it is an absent capability with an honest README deferral. Classified as reframe-deliberate-limitation because the original 'silent stub' framing overstates it — the surface is honestly producer-only with documented deferral — but the underlying observation (no consumer/subscribe surface) is accurate and intentional.

concreteImplOrErrorFound:
Loud error throw: plugins/triggers/src/runtime/trigger-runtime-processor.ts:95-100 throws TriggersError.unsupportedOperation('trigger-action.defer', `Deferred trigger action dispatch is not implemented for ${definition.id}; until=${action.until}.`). UnsupportedOperationError is a real Error subclass (packages/plugin-triggers-core/src/domain/errors.ts:136-149, code TRIGGER_UNSUPPORTED_OPERATION). No silent drop.</concreteImplOrErrorFound>
<parameter name="currentEvidence">["plugins/triggers/src/runtime/trigger-runtime-processor.ts:89-103 — dispatchTriggerAction throws TriggersError.unsupportedOperation('trigger-action.defer', ...) for action.kind === 'defer'; no scheduling/persistence/timer is wired (defer is genuinely not implemented)","packages/plugin-triggers-core/src/domain/errors.ts:135-149 — UnsupportedOperationError extends TriggersError extends Error; sets code TRIGGER_UNSUPPORTED_OPERATION, retryable false — a genuine loud throw, not a renamed stub","plugins/triggers/src/runtime/trigger-runtime-processor_test.ts:17-34 — test 'runtime processor rejects defer actions instead of silently dropping them' asserts result.status === 'dlq', actionsDispatched === 0, one DLQ entry whose reason includes 'Deferred trigger action dispatch is not implemented', idempotency completed and not released. The throw is caught by the processor and routed to DLQ, so defer is captured (not lost) but never dispatched","packages/plugin-triggers-core/src/domain/trigger-action.ts:28-35 — DeferAction { kind: 'defer'; until: string } remains admitted in the exported TriggerActionResult union, so handlers can still emit it; it just always fails to dispatch"]
reasoning: The cited code still exists on current main and matches the gap's evidence. The behavior is a deliberate, honest 'unsupported' limitation, not a silent stub: dispatchTriggerAction throws TriggersError.unsupportedOperation (a real UnsupportedOperationError / TRIGGER_UNSUPPORTED_OPERATION Error subclass), and the processor routes that throw to the DLQ. The test explicitly verifies the action lands in the DLQ (status 'dlq', actionsDispatched 0, idempotency completed, not released) rather than being dropped. This is the reframe pattern: a former silent stub converted into a loud, traceable rejection. I did NOT find a concrete implementation (no scheduler, timer, or deferred-action persistence/re-enqueue), so it is not fixed-on-main. Disposition is document-as-limitation rather than implement: 'defer' is publicly emittable from handlers (it is in the exported union), so users can reach it, but every defer reliably fails loud and is DLQ-captured with a clear message. Building real deferred dispatch is a substantial capability (timer + persistence + replay). Shipping with the loud unsupported error plus a docs note that 'defer' is not yet dispatchable is acceptable for prime-time; severity downgraded to low because there is no data loss or silent failure.

reasoning: On current main the cited lines still throw, but the disposition has materially changed since the original audit. Commit 48ef1783 (S3, PR #70) deliberately converted the former silent no-op into a loud, typed StreamUnsupportedOperationError. The subscribe closure (stream-api.ts:65-67) now throws unsupportedStreamOperation('stream.subscribe'), and the matching producer publish (line 49-50) rejects with the same error class. The error class (lines 17-31) carries a stable .operation identifier and a message directing callers to @netscript/plugin-streams-core runtime primitives; both the class and factory are publicly exported from mod.ts (lines 11-12, 149-151). This is the canonical 'reframe-deliberate-limitation' pattern: defineStreamConsumer/defineStreamProducer are manifest-layer typing/wiring helpers, and runtime subscription/publish belongs to the streams-core service, not these helpers. The throw is honest and discoverable rather than a hidden stub. The one residual wrinkle is that the manifest metadata (mod.ts:119-124) still lists 'SSE and long-poll consumers' as a feature — but that describes the plugin's durable-stream HTTP service, not a promise that the manifest helper performs subscription, and any wording reconciliation is already tracked separately (memory W3b: 'durable streams = unsupported'). That is a docs concern, not a code blocker. Not fixed-on-main (no wired persistence/dispatch was added — subscribe still throws), so this is a deliberate, acceptable shipped limitation. Severity dropped from medium to low because the silent-no-op footgun is gone.

The cited evidence is stale. On current main, deno.json:94 pins '@durable-streams/server' to ^0.3.7 (not the older version that 'only exported DurableStreamTestServer' as an in-memory test backend). In 0.3.7, DurableStreamTestServer.store is typed `StreamStore | FileBackedStreamStore`, and TestServerOptions.dataDir is documented as: 'Data directory for file-backed storage. If provided, enables file-backed mode using LMDB and append-only logs. If omitted, uses in-memory storage.' The package now also exports FileBackedStreamStore, encodeStreamPath/decodeStreamPath, and createRegistryHooks — i.e. real durable persistence primitives, not a test-only stub. plugins/streams/services/src/main.ts NOW wires this: line 31 reads const dataDir = Deno.env.get('STREAMS_DATA_DIR'), and lines 40-44 pass dataDir into the constructor. The module docstring (lines 9-11) and plugins/streams/README.md:87 explicitly document the contract: set STREAMS_DATA_DIR for durable file-backed (LMDB + append-only log) persistence that survives restarts; omit it for in-memory development. So persistence is genuinely implemented and wired through a documented opt-in env var — real dispatch to a FileBackedStreamStore, verified via deno doc npm:@durable-streams/server@0.3.7, not a renamed stub. What remains true: the class is still named DurableStreamTestServer (line 40) and the DEFAULT when STREAMS_DATA_DIR is unset is in-memory (line 31 -> undefined dataDir). But that is now an honest, documented development-mode default rather than a silent production trap, and there is no longer a 'test-server-only / no durable option' problem. The original high-severity framing no longer holds.

Documented in main.ts docstring lines 9-11 and plugins/streams/README.md:87.

reasoning: Re-read all three cited files at current main; git shows no material change since genesis eject so the audit's line numbers hold (stream-api.ts subscribe throw at 61-69, producer reject at 45-52, CLI not-implemented at 59-70, README Deferred Scope at 119-122). This is NOT fixed-on-main: there is no wired runtime persistence or dispatch in this plugin, and by explicit architectural doctrine (README sections 1, 12) this Archetype 5 manifest plugin intentionally does not own runtime stream IO — that belongs to @netscript/plugin-streams-core. However it is also NOT a silent stub: every unsupported path is loud — defineStreamConsumer.subscribe throws a typed StreamUnsupportedOperationError, defineStreamProducer.publish rejects with the same typed error, the CLI returns non-zero exit codes with explicit 'not implemented until the streams runtime CLI is wired' messages, and list-topics returns an explicit walker-not-implemented result. The README documents the entire boundary in a dedicated Deferred Scope section. This is a deliberate, honestly-surfaced, documented limitation rather than a real correctness defect. Disposition is document-as-limitation, not implement: building runtime publish/subscribe into this manifest-tier plugin would violate the stated package boundary (runtime primitives belong in streams-core). Severity low because the limitation is already loud and documented; nothing silently misleads a caller.

The KV-polling and redis adapters use a DIFFERENT, nested set of knobs (connection.denoKv.maxRetries / retryBaseDelay), not the top-level QueueOptions retry fields. So the documented promise is unfulfilled: the values are silently accepted and discarded. Neither remedy exists: (a) no concrete wiring/dispatch of these options, and (b) no loud unsupported-operation throw converting the silent stub into an honest limitation.

concreteImplOrErrorFound:
No fix and no loud error. All three adapters still perform the unsafe cast `$queryRaw(query as unknown as TemplateStringsArray, ...params)`. Current-main line numbers (unchanged from audit): postgres.adapter.ts:156-163, mssql.adapter.ts:489-496, mysql.adapter.ts:426-433. Port contract database-client.ts:119 promises `executeRaw(query: string, ...params)`. The sibling `executeRawUnsafe` correctly routes to `$executeRawUnsafe`, but `executeRaw` does NOT route to `$queryRawUnsafe`.</concreteImplOrErrorFound>
<parameter name="currentEvidence">["packages/database/adapters/postgres.adapter.ts:156-163 — executeRaw still does `return $queryRaw(query as unknown as TemplateStringsArray, ...params)`","packages/database/adapters/mssql.adapter.ts:489-496 — identical unsafe cast, unchanged","packages/database/adapters/mysql.adapter.ts:426-433 — identical unsafe cast, unchanged","packages/database/ports/database-client.ts:119 — interface contract: `executeRaw<T>(query: string, ...params: unknown[]): Promise<T>` (plain string + params, not a tagged template)","No StreamUnsupportedOperationError / notImplemented() / unsupportedOperation throw anywhere in these methods — the silent broken cast remains"]
reasoning: Re-read on current main: the three cited methods are byte-for-byte the same as the audit described, at the exact same line numbers. Prisma's `$queryRaw` is a tagged-template function that reads `strings.raw` and interleaves `...values` as bound parameters. A plain string has no `.raw` (undefined) and indexes to characters, so the cast yields broken behavior; worse, the trailing `...params` are not bound as parameterized placeholders the way a genuine `$queryRaw` template binds them, so callers passing params get incorrect/unsafe queries. The correct primitive for a string-plus-params API is `$queryRawUnsafe(query, ...params)` (which the adapter already imports/uses pattern for executeRawUnsafe). There is no new concrete implementation fulfilling the promise and no loud unsupported-operation error converting this into an honest limitation. It is neither fixed-on-main nor a deliberate reframe — it remains a silent broken/unsafe stub.

reasoning: On current main the gap is reframed, not fixed by real capability. The former silent stub (core returning status:'deferred' with no scheduling) is now converted into a deliberate, loud, non-retryable throw in the deployed runtime: dispatchTriggerAction throws TriggersError.unsupportedOperation('trigger-action.defer'). There is NO wired persistence/scheduler for deferred actions — nothing actually re-dispatches at action.until — so this is not fixed-on-main; it is an honest 'unsupported' declaration. The core processor's status:'deferred' branch is dead in the real runtime because every action is dispatched (and thus throws) before status is computed. The cited doc path docs/site/reference/triggers/index.md no longer exists (docs overhaul moved user docs off this tree), so the specific docs-vs-impl mismatch evidence is stale. Throwing a loud non-retryable error for an unbuilt durable-scheduling capability is an acceptable shipped limitation; building a deferred-action scheduler is out of prime-time scope. Severity drops to low: the runtime is honest (loud throw), and the only residual is reconciling the published DeferAction doc-comment / docs to say 'defer dispatch is unsupported', which is tracked by pending task #35 — a documentation reconciliation, not a missing-implementation blocker.

Re-verified against current main. The gap holds on all three legs:

(1) Config defines queueProvider but no runtime consumes it. packages/plugin-workers-core/src/config/workers-config.ts:34 declares QueueProviderData ('auto'|'deno-kv'|'redis'|'postgres'|'amqp'); :43 the WorkersConfigData.queueProvider field; :106 the zod schema binding. A repo-wide grep for `queueProvider` returns ONLY: this config file, two sample/test configs (plugins/workers/src/cli/official-sample-configuration.ts:212 'auto', packages/cli/.../plugin-loader_test.ts:138 'auto'), and the prime-time register backlog note. No runtime code reads it.

(2) Every worker-runtime createQueue call drops the provider. plugins/workers/worker/worker.ts:174 `this.queue = createQueue<JobMessage>(this.queueName)`; scheduler.ts:94 identical; queue-consumer.ts:35 `createQueue<unknown>(config.queueName)` and :54 `createQueue<TaskMessage>('tasks')`; services router-context.ts:40,47 also bare. None pass the QueueOptions.provider field, so the configured backend is ignored and the queue falls through to auto-discovery.

(3) Postgres is never auto-selected. packages/queue/factory/create-queue.ts:149-162 detectProvider() checks only RabbitMQ (isServiceAvailable) > Redis (getRedisConnectionFromEnv) > DenoKv default. Postgres has a full createPostgresQueue adapter (:328-346) and is handled in createQueueFactory (:221) and getNativeRetrial (:242), but it is reachable ONLY via an explicit options.provider — which no worker call site supplies. So a user setting queueProvider:'postgres' still silently gets DenoKv.

No StreamUnsupportedOperationError / unsupportedOperation / notImplemented() throw exists for this path — the config value is silently discarded, not loudly rejected. So this is neither fixed-on-main nor a reframed deliberate limitation. The .llm/tmp/framework-prime-time-register.md:320 backlog entry ("Thread resolved workers queueProvider into createQueue and add Postgres auto-discovery") confirms it remains unimplemented work.
concreteImplOrErrorFound: No implementation and no loud error. queueProvider config is accepted and silently ignored: worker.ts:174, scheduler.ts:94, queue-consumer.ts:35/54 all call createQueue(name) with no provider option; detectProvider (create-queue.ts:149-162) omits Postgres entirely.
